---
title: "Generative Production"
subtitle: "The study guide, presented"
---

<!--
Authoring convention for this deck:

  Slide body     = the audience view — condensed, visual, memorable.
  ::: {.notes}   = presenter notes — the full detail, organized as talking points.
                   Always start with `**Source:** <relative path to the wiki page>`
                   so the speaker can jump to the long-form version and the editor
                   of either file knows where the other one lives.

  Edit wiki page substantively? Skim the matching slide and its notes — sync them.
  Edit a slide? Follow its Source: link back to the wiki and confirm.
-->

# Vibe-coding for Filmmakers

[Code is generative production, too.]{.fragment}

::: {.notes}
**Source:** `vibe-coding/index.md`

The umbrella of the section. One question organizes everything here:
*how do we keep beginner coders from making big mistakes?*

Writing code with AI coding tools is the same move as prompting an image
model — prompt, get candidates, select, iterate, track — with different
outputs. This section covers the code-specific skin over that shared skill.
:::

## Working with Models

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
### Prompting, context, the right model for the job. {.fragment}
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](assets/model-constellation.svg){fig-alt="A central prompt node connected to four candidate outputs by dashed lines." width="100%"}
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
**Source:** `vibe-coding/working-with-models/index.md`

Topics to cover:

- Coding models compared — Opus / Sonnet / Haiku, GPT-5, Gemini, local models.
  Strengths, context windows, cost.
- Prompting for code — refines the universal prompting principles for the
  coding case: constraints, examples, iterative refinement.
- Context management — why "start a new chat" matters; `CLAUDE.md` /
  `AGENTS.md` / rules files.
- Skills — what they are, when to build one.
- MCP — what it is, when it's worth wiring up.
- Agents vs chat vs autocomplete — different affordances.

Key takeaway: the chat window is an *environment*, not a prompt box.
Curate it.
:::

## Building Software

### Boring tech wins. {.fragment}

::: {.notes}
**Source:** `vibe-coding/building-software/index.md`

Sensible defaults for someone assembling their first non-trivial project.

- Reading API docs — REST basics, SDKs vs raw HTTP.
- Frameworks vs rolling-your-own — when to trust a boring framework
  vs let the agent improvise.
- Boring tech wins — Rails, Next.js, and the other old standbys beat
  bespoke scaffolding.
- Dependencies and package managers — why random packages are risky.
- Reading error messages — understand before pasting to the model.

Key takeaway: the framework you didn't invent is almost always better than
the framework the agent improvised in the last ten minutes.
:::

## Common Traps

### *Commit a secret. Force-push. Trust the demo.* {.fragment}

A named gallery of specific beginner mistakes, with the fix for each.

::: {.notes}
**Source:** `vibe-coding/common-traps/index.md`

The "keep beginners safe" promise made concrete. Each trap is a short
cautionary tale ending with the fix.

Planned pages:

- Committing secrets to git.
- Blindly running agent-suggested shell commands.
- Force-push disasters and losing work.
- Letting the agent refactor without review.
- Accepting the first thing that compiles.
- Scope-creep prompts.
- Building on sand — unreliable deps, beta APIs, deprecated models.
- Runaway API bills.
- Deploying to production without testing.
- Trusting a local-running model demo when the finished product needs
  reliability.
- "Works on my machine" illusions.

Cross-linked to `practical/` where topics overlap.
:::

## Foundations (Vibe-coding)

### Agents · Code · Terminal · Git {.fragment}

::: {.notes}
**Source:** `vibe-coding/foundations/index.md`

Mental models for someone who's coded little or not at all.
The on-ramp before the setup and safety material.

- What is an agent? — IDE vs chat vs autocomplete; different affordances
  for different jobs.
- Where does code actually run? — local, cloud, hybrid, and why it matters
  for cost and trust.
- Terminal literacy — `cd`, `ls`, what a shell is, why the agent drops you
  into one.
- Git literacy — why version control matters *especially* for AI-generated
  code.
:::

## Setup and Safety

### The things that hurt you if you skip them. {.fragment}

Credentials. Billing. What never goes into git.

::: {.notes}
**Source:** `vibe-coding/setup-and-safety/index.md`

Planned topics:

- Managing credentials — `.env` files, never committing secrets,
  password-manager patterns.
- API keys — what they are, how billing works, rate limits, spending caps.
- Repos and `.gitignore` — what *never* goes into a public repo.
- Sandboxing — when to trust an agent to run commands.

Key takeaway: set up `.gitignore` *before* you put a credential in a file.
:::

## Managing Credentials

### *Leak one and you pay for someone else's bill.* {.fragment}

[**Password manager → .env → .gitignore.**]{.fragment}

::: {.notes}
**Source:** `vibe-coding/setup-and-safety/managing-credentials.md`

Credentials are the secrets your code uses to access paid services.
Key leaks are the single most common way beginner coders get burned —
a public GitHub repo with a key in it gets scraped within minutes.

Key ideas to walk through:

- **Never commit a credential.** Not in code, not in config, not in a
  comment, not "just temporarily." Git remembers.
- **`.env` + environment variables.** `KEY=value` lines. Code reads from
  the environment. `.env` is gitignored; ship a `.env.example` with blank
  values.
- **Password manager is source of truth.** 1Password, Bitwarden, Apple
  Passwords. `.env` is a convenience cache.
- **Rotate on leak — don't just delete the commit.** Git history is
  distributed; you don't know who cloned the repo in the window the key
  was public.
- **Cap spending at the vendor.** Most AI vendors let you set a hard
  monthly limit. Set one. Leaked uncapped keys have cost beginners
  thousands of dollars in a day.

Common mistakes to name:

- Committing a `.env` file.
- Pasting a real key into a chat or example.
- Reusing one key for everything.
- Leaving a leaked key live because "I deleted the commit."

Exercise: before writing a line of API-using code, do the full setup —
`.env`, `.gitignore`, `.env.example`, `git status` to confirm, store the
real key in the password manager.
:::

# Practical

[The non-creative stuff that sinks projects.]{.fragment}

::: {.notes}
**Source:** `practical/index.md`

Licenses, cost, reproducibility, likeness, provenance, delivery.
Applies across the whole site, not just one section.

This chapter's slides are not authored yet — pull audience attention back
to the wiki for these topics, or add slide pages as the prose solidifies.
:::
